trajectory.description: Observation: Baseline with ConvNeXt-Tiny (10 epochs, mixup+label smoothing) achieved 74.84% test accuracy. Val acc peaked at epoch 5 then declined, indicating overfitting to noisy labels. Analysis: With ~40% label noise, standard training memorizes noisy labels after warmup. Need noise-aware training (DivideMix-style) that uses GMM to separate clean/noisy samples and applies different losses. Also need EMA for stable predictions and TTA for better inference. Decision: Implement DivideMix-style training with EMA, cosine LR schedule, and 25 epochs (5 warmup + 20 noise-aware).

trajectory.description: Observation: DivideMix failed catastrophically (1.6%), likely due to bugs in soft label/GMM pipeline. Simple ConvNeXt + mixup achieved 74.84%. Analysis: Complex noise-aware methods are fragile; simpler approaches with strong regularization are more reliable. Decision: Write clean two-phase training: Phase 1 (20 epochs, ConvNeXt + mixup/cutmix/label_smoothing/EMA) + Phase 2 (10 epochs, self-training with label correction from Phase 1). Also update predict.py with better TTA.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iterations 2-3 failed catastrophically (1.2-1.6% accuracy) while iteration 1's simple approach got 74.84%. The root cause is the LR scheduler bug: scheduler.step() was called per-batch but T_max was set to num_epochs (not total_steps), causing the cosine schedule to cycle through ~23 full cycles per epoch, driving LR to near-zero almost immediately. This explains why val_acc stayed at ~1% (random for 100 classes) despite declining train_loss. Analysis: Need to fix the scheduler by using LambdaLR with proper step counting (total_steps = steps_per_epoch * num_epochs). Also, need to save EMA weights (not training weights) as the model_state_dict in the checkpoint. Decision: Write clean training script with proper cosine LR schedule + warmup, ConvNeXt-Tiny, mixup/cutmix/label_smoothing/EMA, train 30 epochs, submit eval immediately.

Observation: Best accuracy 80.32% with ConvNeXt-Tiny + mixup/LS/EMA. Iterations 2-3 failed due to scheduler bugs (now fixed). Analysis: With 40% label noise, self-training can help by using warmup model's predictions to downweight/correct noisy labels. Decision: Implement self-training - load warmup model, generate soft labels with TTA, train with confidence-weighted loss combining hard and soft labels. Fine-tune from warmup weights for 30 epochs.

Observation: Best accuracy is 80.32% from iteration 4 (ConvNeXt + mixup/LS/EMA). Self-training in iteration 6 didn't improve (val_acc=0.5978 vs 0.5992). Analysis: The key issue is 40% label noise. Self-training with soft labels was too uncertain (mean confidence 0.41). A more principled approach is needed: use cleanlab (pre-installed) to identify noisy samples, then train with sample reweighting + label correction on high-confidence disagreements. Decision: Implement cleanlab noise detection + sample reweighting + strong augmentation training. Save old best.pt as backup, train new model, and submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Best accuracy is 80.32% from iteration 4 (ConvNeXt + mixup/LS/EMA). Cleanlab (iter 7) and self-training (iter 6) both failed (~60% val_acc). Time remaining: ~30 min. Analysis: The simple regularization approach works best. To beat 80.32%, ensembling two diverse models is the most promising approach. I'll train a new ConvNeXt-Tiny model from scratch with different seed/hyperparams for 12 epochs (~18 min), then ensemble with the existing best.pt in predict.py. Submitting eval with best.pt as primary (guaranteed fallback to 80.32%) and best_v2.pt as secondary for ensembling.

trajectory.description: Observation: TTA with 8 transforms degraded accuracy from 80.32% to 78.28%. Heavy TTA is hurting because the diverse transforms introduce distribution shift. The best result (80.32%) was likely achieved with simpler inference. Time remaining: ~9 min. Analysis: Need to simplify predict.py to use just basic resize + center crop inference, no TTA flips or multi-scale crops. Decision: Rewrite predict.py with minimal inference (just resize + normalize), submit eval with best.pt immediately.

python -c "
import torch

for f in sorted(os.listdir('checkpoints')):

        val_acc = ckpt.get('val_acc', 'N/A')
        epoch = ckpt.get('epoch', 'N/A')
        keys = list(ckpt.keys())
        print(f'{f}: val_acc={val_acc}, epoch={epoch}, keys={keys[:5]}')
    except Exception as e:
        print(f'{f}: ERROR {e}')
"

trajectory.description: Observation: All checkpoints have val_acc ~0.59-0.60 on noisy val set. cleanlab_best.pt has highest val_acc (0.6018). Best test accuracy was 80.32% with best.pt. Time critically low (~8 min). Analysis: Ensembling multiple trained models by averaging logits is the most reliable way to boost accuracy with no training time. cleanlab_best.pt likely has better clean-label accuracy than best.pt despite similar noisy val_acc. Decision: Create ensemble predict.py that averages logits from cleanlab_best.pt + warmup_v1.pt + best_v2.pt + best.pt, submit eval with cleanlab_best.pt as primary checkpoint.
